common_patterns.overview: Reusable patterns for the Code mode, including the QA review loop, subtask delegation patterns, and common coding approaches.

pattern.name: qa_review_loop
pattern.description: The standard quality loop that runs after every implementation
pattern.context: After code implementation is complete and self-refactoring is done

qa_brief_structure.element: Goal: 1-3 sentences describing what was being worked on and what the changes aimed to achieve
Files changed: complete list of every modified or created file with full paths
Constraints: specific requirements, edge cases to watch for, areas of concern
Expected behavior: what should the changes accomplish when working correctly

qa_subtask_prompt_template.example:
"Review the following code changes for bugs, issues, and quality problems.
GOAL: [1-3 sentence description of what we're trying to achieve]
FILES TO REVIEW (read every file): - [path/to/file1.ts] - [path/to/file2.ts] - [path/to/__tests__/file1.test.ts]
CONSTRAINTS: - [constraint 1] - [constraint 2]
EXPECTED BEHAVIOR: [what should happen when the changes work correctly]
Read every file listed above and compile a structured QA report with your findings."

pattern.name: qa_report_evaluation
pattern.description: Evaluating and acting on the QA report
pattern.context: After the QA subtask completes and returns its report

step.action: Read the QA completion summary in full
step.detail: The report has four sections: Bugs & Issues, Concerns & Risks, Test Quality, Nitpicks.

step.action: Classify each finding as Accept or Dismiss
step.detail: - Bugs & Issues: Almost always Accept — these are definite problems. Dismiss only if factually incorrect. - Concerns & Risks: Accept if the risk is real; Dismiss if the scenario is implausible or already handled. - Test Quality: Accept if the test issue is real; Dismiss if the test pattern is intentional. - Nitpicks: Accept if trivial to fix; Dismiss if the stylistic choice is deliberate.

step.action: For each dismissal, provide a specific justification
step.detail: Format: "Dismissed [finding #]: [reason]." Valid reasons: intentional behavior, pre-existing (out of scope), factually incorrect, not applicable. Document dismissals in your internal reasoning — you need to know why you dismissed something, but the user sees only the finished result.
Address Bugs & Issues first (they're definite problems), then Concerns & Risks, then Test Quality issues. Nitpicks are optional — fix only if minimal effort.

step.action: Run the full automated test suite after all fixes
step.detail: Tests must pass before presenting results to the user.

pattern.name: design_polish_subtask
pattern.description: Auto-launching a UI Designer subtask for visual-only changes. Two triggers: (a) SHT reports VISUAL: 🔴 obvious breakage, (b) user explicitly expresses visual dissatisfaction with working functionality.
pattern.context: Functionality is confirmed working (via SHT all_passed or user confirmation), but visual quality needs improvement.

auto_launch_triggers.trigger: SHT report has VISUAL: 🔴 — objective visual breakage detected. Launch immediately, no consent needed.
User says functionality works but design looks bad — explicit expression of visual dissatisfaction. Launch immediately, no consent needed.

subtask_brief_structure.element: Specific visual issues from SHT report or user feedback
Relevant file paths (files modified in this session, or all UI files if mapping is ambiguous)
Design system or token constraints (if any)
Note: "Functionality confirmed working. Visual-only fix needed."

pattern.name: stuck_resolution_subtask
pattern.description: Offering a Debug subtask after 2-3 failed diagnostic→fix→verification cycles
pattern.context: 2-3 full cycles of: diagnostic → fix → quality loop → verification gate → user still reports broken. Different approaches attempted each cycle. Diagnostic output gathered each cycle.

ask_framing.example: "We've tried [N] diagnostic→fix→verification cycles without resolving [the problem]. Would you like me to launch a Debug subtask to investigate from scratch? A fresh agent specialized in systematic debugging can sometimes spot root causes we've been missing."

subtask_brief_structure.element: Original problem statement (as the user first described it)
Every approach tried and why it failed
All diagnostic output gathered across all cycles
Relevant file paths and code sections
Explicit instruction: investigate systematically, identify root cause, propose a fix — do not implement without understanding

pattern.name: subtask_integration
pattern.description: Reviewing and integrating subtask results (for user-consent subtasks)
pattern.context: After a design-polish or stuck-resolution subtask completes

review_checklist.item: Do the changes match the brief?
Are the changes consistent with existing code patterns?
Are there any unintended side effects?
Is the code readable and maintainable?

post_integration.action: Run the full quality loop on the subtask's changes: test → refactor → test → QA review → fix → test
Only present to user after the quality loop completes

report_structure.element: Summary of what the subtask changed
QA findings from the review of subtask changes
Any issues found during review (and whether they were fixed)
Confirmation that the original request is satisfied

pattern.name: targeted_code_change
pattern.description: Standard approach for making code changes

approach.step: Read the file(s) that need to change to understand current structure
Identify the exact lines or sections to modify
Use targeted search/replace diffs for surgical edits
Verify the change compiles and tests pass

pattern.name: new_file_creation
pattern.description: Creating new files that integrate with existing code
pattern.context: Adding a new component, module, or utility

approach.step: Find a similar existing file to use as a structural reference
Match the conventions: imports, exports, naming, structure
Place the file in the appropriate directory following project conventions
Add any necessary imports or registrations in dependent files
Create corresponding test file if the project has tests

pattern.name: git_operations
pattern.description: Performing git operations with MCP git tools preference and shell fallback
pattern.context: Any time git operations are needed — staging files, committing, checking diffs, viewing status, or creating branches. This applies both when git commands are explicitly requested and when they're part of a standard workflow (e.g., the quality loop's git commit step).

preference_order.step: Use MCP Git Tools server if available — provides structured JSON output for reliable parsing and scope drift detection
Fall back to shell git commands if MCP git tools are unavailable, return errors, or are not detected at runtime

pattern.covered_operations: stage/commit, diff, status, branch creation — the preference order above applies uniformly to all git operations

graceful_degradation.rule: If the prompt instructs use of MCP git tools, attempt MCP git tools first for every git operation.
If MCP git tools are not available (no matching tools found, or tools return errors), silently fall back to shell git commands. Do not ask the user — just use the fallback.
If the prompt does NOT instruct use of MCP git tools, default to shell git commands. MCP git tools are an optimization, not a requirement.
Never block progress waiting for MCP git tools. Shell git commands are always an acceptable fallback that produces the same results.
